title: Hand To God

jokes.text: George Michael's deadpan response when asked if he's alone: 'Almost always, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if George Michael is alone, expecting a simple yes or no.
jokes.punchline: 'Almost always' — a quietly devastating admission of his social isolation delivered with total flatness.
jokes.explanation: Three words that perfectly encapsulate George Michael's entire existence — lonely, self-aware, understated. The economy is exceptional; any more words would ruin it. It's deeply character-specific and earns its laugh through truth, not a trick.

jokes.text: Ann's religious group is trying to get Nip/Tuck off the air. 'Ironically, she likes Gangy.'
jokes.setup: Ann's group objects to Nip/Tuck because 'God wants people to age naturally.'
jokes.punchline: She makes an exception for Gangy (Lucille), a heavily botoxed woman — the irony is rich and character-specific.
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: a jab at religious hypocrisy and a running gag about Lucille's vanity. The word 'Gangy' is funny in itself. Strong economy and clever ironic structure.

jokes.text: Michael says he'd never date anyone without George Michael being okay with it. George Michael: 'You don't have to do that.' Michael: 'Great, because I didn't do it this time.'
jokes.setup: Michael sets up a touching, considerate declaration about not dating without his son's blessing.
jokes.punchline: He immediately reveals he's already done it — the declaration was retroactively meaningless and he was fishing for absolution.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully compressed bait-and-switch. Michael's self-delusion as 'the good guy' is perfectly weaponized here. The structure is flawless: the setup is warm, the reversal is instantaneous, and it reveals character. One of the episode's stronger dialogue jokes.

jokes.text: George Michael on Maggie: 'Didn't she turn out to not be blind though?' Michael: 'Exactly.'
jokes.setup: Michael announces he reconnected with 'that blind prosecutor' Maggie Lizer.
jokes.punchline: George Michael correctly notes she wasn't blind. Michael's 'Exactly' treats 'not actually blind' as a known and acceptable quirk rather than a disqualifying deception.
jokes.explanation: Michael's 'Exactly' does enormous comedic heavy lifting — it's both a callback recap for the audience and a character indictment. His total non-concern about being deceived reads as a flaw. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael hadn't seen her in eight and a half months, during which she had filled out.' George Michael: 'Really? Could have sworn it was a year.'
jokes.setup: The narrator says Maggie 'had filled out' — implying pregnancy weight. George Michael questions the timeline of eight and a half months.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's correction implies he's doing pregnancy math, innocently suggesting the timeline is too short for Michael to be the father.
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in layered Arrested Development comedy. The narrator's euphemism sets up one joke, but George Michael's oblivious math creates a second, deeper joke: he's accidentally doing paternity arithmetic. The rewatch bonus is high because you only fully appreciate what he's saying once you know the episode's twist. Excellent structure using narrator/character interplay.

jokes.text: Michael says Maggie might not really be pregnant because she lies. George Michael: 'Boy, you really are my kid.' Michael: 'Good. Apparently, so is this one.'
jokes.setup: Michael affirms the father-son bond with George Michael after they think alike about Maggie's deception.
jokes.punchline: Michael pivots 'kid' to mean the unborn child — a rapid double meaning that also undercuts the touching moment.
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay with good structural timing — the warm beat is established and then immediately punctured with the double meaning of 'kid.' Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Lindsay and Tobias broke into Maggie's house to hide in her bathroom and collect/test her urine.
jokes.setup: The narrator explains how Michael found out Maggie was really pregnant.
jokes.punchline: The method — breaking into someone's home to secretly collect and test their urine — is presented with complete matter-of-fact normalcy.
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from the narrator's breezy delivery of a completely deranged plan. Classic Arrested Development tonal joke — the absurdity is the normalcy.

jokes.text: Maggie: 'Oh, you're so right, Michael, 'cause I'm fakin' it—' Narrator: 'But he was proven wrong.' Maggie: '—Not.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronted Maggie about the fat suit found in her bathroom, suggesting she's faking the pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Maggie begins to seemingly confirm she's faking, the narrator cuts in to say Michael was wrong, and then Maggie completes her sentence with '—Not.' The narrator and character are in dialogue.
jokes.explanation: The structural ingenuity here is exceptional: the sentence is split across a narrator interruption, creating a triple-fake. The '90s 'Not!' callback gives it a comedic jolt. The narrator actively participates in the joke's structure, which is a signature AD move. Rewatch value is high because you realize on second viewing exactly how many layers of lying are in play.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I don't know which one.' Oscar: 'The hand.' Narrator: 'I do.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says Buster lost 'part of his body' and doesn't know which one. Oscar helpfully supplies 'The hand.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator chimes in with 'I do' — entering the scene as if he's a participant and one-upping Oscar's correction with smug omniscience.
jokes.explanation: The narrator breaking the fourth wall to compete with in-scene characters is a signature AD move executed perfectly here. Two words — 'I do' — accomplish an enormous amount: they're funny as a narrator power play, they mock Lucille's affected ignorance, and they establish Arrested Development's comedic universe. Economy score is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Buster was attacked by 'a loose seal' — audio of seal barking.
jokes.setup: Narrator explains that Buster went swimming and was attacked.
jokes.punchline: 'A loose seal' — homophone for 'Lucille,' Buster's domineering mother, who is effectively responsible for his attack. The pun is earned by the plot.
jokes.explanation: One of the most celebrated jokes in the show's history. 'Loose seal' = 'Lucille' is a pun that works on every level: phonetically, thematically (Lucille's control is literally dangerous), and narratively (Gob released the seal, Buster is the victim of maternal control). It's a pun that's also a profound character statement. The series paid this off across multiple seasons. This is a legitimate contender for a 9+ score.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'If only your father was here.' Oscar: 'Oh, that George. God only knows where he is.' Buster (from attic window): 'The car!'
jokes.setup: Lucille laments that George Sr. isn't there. Oscar performs ignorance about his location.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. shouts from wherever he's hiding — in this case the car he stole — immediately revealing Oscar's performative ignorance.
jokes.explanation: A solid ironic joke in the Bluth tradition — everyone pretends not to know where the fugitive patriarch is, immediately undercut by him yelling his location. The layering of Lucille's faux-longing and Oscar's fake ignorance is well-constructed.

jokes.text: George Sr. test-driving a Ford Escape from a dealership, yelling 'Up yours, granny!' while Lucille echoes 'You couldn't handle it!'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is a fugitive who has snuck out of hiding to go to a car dealership.
jokes.punchline: He asks to test-drive 'The Escape' — a car whose name perfectly describes what he's doing — and the scene cuts to him shouting out the window at elderly pedestrians.
jokes.explanation: The Ford Escape/escape pun is well-timed, but the main laugh is the absurdity of a fugitive's first act of freedom being road rage against an old woman. The parallel Lucille echo is a nice structural touch.

jokes.text: George Sr. at dealership: 'Yeah, the Bronco's been discontinued. We're trying to shed that whole "fugitive on the run" thing.' Salesman: 'This is the Escape.' George Sr.: 'What a fun name, hmm?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. comes into a dealership and the salesman notes the Bronco is discontinued due to O.J. Simpson associations.
jokes.punchline: The Escape is presented as the alternative, and George Sr. — an actual fugitive — savors the name with obvious personal meaning while playing dumb.
jokes.explanation: The Bronco/O.J. reference gives this cultural resonance while the Escape gag layers perfectly. George Sr.'s 'What a fun name, hmm?' is a beautifully self-satisfied reading. The joke works on multiple registers: meta-commentary on Ford's PR, O.J. reference, and George Sr.'s fugitive status all collide. Strong originality and character integration.

jokes.text: Michael tells George Michael he'll have to be the 'baby of the family.' George Michael: 'Um, Maeby's younger than me.' Michael: 'You're never going in the ocean! Never!'
jokes.setup: Michael, distracted, tries to comfort George Michael about Buster's injury by promoting him to 'baby of the family.'
jokes.punchline: George Michael correctly notes Maeby is younger, but Michael's already been swept into a Lucille-like hysterical prohibition ('You're never going in the ocean!'), completely ignoring the logical objection.
jokes.explanation: A good character joke — Michael momentarily becomes Lucille, making irrational parental pronouncements about ocean safety while ignoring a perfectly reasonable objection. The character mirroring is the real joke.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Not Dr. Wordsmith.' Then: Doctor says Buster 'is going to be all right' meaning he still has his right hand, not that he'll recover. 'He's lost his left hand, so he's going to be "all right."'
jokes.setup: The Bluths anxiously ask how Buster is. The doctor says 'He's going to be all right.' The family takes this as good news. Lindsay: 'Finally some good news from this guy.'
jokes.punchline: The doctor clarifies: he literally means Buster will be 'all right' — he lost his left hand. Lucille: 'You son of a bitch! I hate this doctor!'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's crown jewel. The setup is immaculate — 'he's going to be all right' reads as reassurance, the family's relief is earned, and Lindsay's 'finally some good news from this guy' perfectly deepens the trap before the pun is revealed. Lucille's 'You son of a bitch! I hate this doctor!' is the ideal capper. The joke layers wordplay, misdirection, character reaction, and even a callback to the doctor's name ('Dr. Wordsmith') that was planted moments earlier. Among the best jokes in the series. Comparable to the 9.5 anchor calibration.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Actually, I'm relieved. I don't have to go to army because the seal with the bow tie attacked me. I feel like I've lost three pounds.'
jokes.setup: Everyone expects Buster to be devastated about losing his hand.
jokes.punchline: Buster is relieved because it gets him out of military service. His 'I feel like I've lost three pounds' is a matter-of-fact observation about having literally lost a hand.
jokes.explanation: Deeply character-specific — only Buster, who has been terrified of being sent to war, would be relieved at losing a limb. The 'three pounds' line is a dark, physically literal joke that works perfectly. The subverted expectation (grief → relief) is excellently structured.

jokes.text: Buster mentions 'the seal with the bow tie' that attacked him. Lucille: 'Seal with the bow tie?'
jokes.setup: Buster describes his attacker as a seal wearing a bow tie.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's incredulous repeat plants the seed of Gob's guilt — the audience already knows Gob dressed a seal in a bow tie.
jokes.explanation: A crucial plot-comedy junction. The detail is absurd on its face ('seal with a bow tie') but also the mechanism by which Gob's culpability will be revealed. Strong structural function within the episode's running gag architecture.

jokes.text: Buster: 'And my hand is off to you.' Then hysterical laughter from himself, forced laughter from Michael.
jokes.setup: Michael says 'My hat is off to you' complimenting Buster's resilience about losing his hand.
jokes.punchline: Buster substitutes 'hand' for 'hat' — making the dark joke about his own amputation, then laughs hysterically while Michael forces a polite laugh.
jokes.explanation: The combination of the pun itself and the performance beats (Buster's hysterical laughter, Michael's forced laugh) elevates this beyond a simple wordplay joke into a cringe masterpiece. Buster's emotional extremism — laughing maniacally at his own dismemberment — is deeply characteristic.

jokes.text: Buster: 'A seal didn't bite your Linus off, did it?' Michael: 'No, a seal didn't bite my Linus off.'
jokes.setup: Buster is urging Michael to pursue Maggie and not give up on the baby.
jokes.punchline: Buster uses 'Linus' as a euphemism for a body part — apparently a natural reference for him — and Michael has to solemnly confirm that no, he hasn't suffered that particular seal-related injury.
jokes.explanation: The joke succeeds on Buster's characteristic innocence combined with unexpected vulgarity. The earnestness of Michael's confirmation — delivered straight — is the real punchline. Classic Buster absurdism.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Then go to her. Ask for her hand.' Michael: 'And maybe I'll get one for my brother while I'm at it.'
jokes.setup: Buster encourages Michael to ask Maggie for 'her hand' in the romantic sense.
jokes.punchline: Michael riffs on 'hand' literally — he'll pick up a spare hand for Buster — dark humor about the amputation treated as a shopping errand.
jokes.explanation: A solid hand pun with dark undertones. Michael's pivot from romantic metaphor to literal amputation-supply-run is quick and effective. The casualness of the delivery is key.

jokes.text: Buster's hook (temporary prosthetic) gets caught and causes him to scream. Everyone panics. 'Whose idea was the temporary hand?'
jokes.setup: Buster has been fitted with a temporary prosthetic. The scene has been comedically calm.
jokes.punchline: The prosthetic gets caught, causing screaming chaos, ending with the exasperated 'Whose idea was the temporary hand?'
jokes.explanation: Functional slapstick escalation that works in the moment. The exasperated capper line is good but the physical comedy is mostly implied through dialogue. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Buster's sudden screaming outburst after the laughing — 'Get the [bleep] out of my room!'
jokes.setup: The hospital scene is winding down on a wave of dark hand jokes and group laughter
jokes.punchline: Buster abruptly screams in pain/rage, shattering the lightness — the audience realizes the joking masked real trauma
jokes.explanation: A sharp tonal pivot that uses the dark comedy register of the scene against itself. The sudden outburst is both a comedic beat (the bleep, the absurdity) and a genuine character moment. Efficiently executed.

jokes.text: Barry the lawyer: 'She's in the bar. What a spanking I gave her in court today.' Then he spanks Michael. Michael: 'Did you spank her?' Barry: 'No!'
jokes.setup: Barry describes winning a case against Maggie using the word 'spanking.'
jokes.punchline: He then literally spanks Michael on the rear, as if the word triggered the action. The judge later warns Barry about touching — 'You said spanking.'
jokes.explanation: Barry's inappropriate physical behavior is consistent character comedy. The callback in the courtroom scene ('You said spanking') rewards the setup. Good character integration — this is very Barry Zuckerkorn.

jokes.text: Michael confesses the break-in to police officers who are currently investigating... the break-in he's confessing to.
jokes.setup: Officers arrive to tell Maggie there's no sign of a break-in and that she should call them back if there's a problem.
jokes.punchline: Michael blurts out 'Hey, the break-in? I told you that was us.' — confessing to the crime to the officers actively investigating it.
jokes.explanation: Classic Bluth self-incrimination played straight. Michael's inability to stop talking when he should is a recurring character trait, and the irony of confessing to investigators who just said there was no sign of a break-in is well-structured.

jokes.text: Michael on the potential Harvard sperm donor father: 'He's probably some geek Simpsons writer's kid.'
jokes.setup: Maggie reveals the biological father is an anonymous Harvard graduate from a sperm bank.
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate association of 'Harvard geek' with Simpsons writers is both a meta-jab at the rival show (AD and The Simpsons are both Fox properties) and a self-aware Hollywood insider joke.
jokes.explanation: A sharp meta-joke that uses Fox network rivalry for comedy. 'Geek Simpsons writer' is pointed and specific in a way that generic celebrity name-drops aren't. The later callback where Lindsay defends Simpsons writers deepens it. This is punching in a specific, earned way.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael had always thought of himself as that great a guy. The kind of guy who could raise someone else's baby.' Michael: 'Of course I am.' Narrator: 'But he wasn't — and he regretted it the moment he said it.' Visual of Michael mouthing silent words.
jokes.setup: Maggie asks if Michael is 'really that great a guy' to raise another man's child. Michael confidently says yes.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately undermines him, and the visual of Michael's silent mouthing of regret makes it a complete character destruction in real time.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best sustained jokes — it takes Michael's entire self-image (the good guy) and collapses it in two sentences, made devastating by the narrator's immediate certainty. The visual reaction beat of Michael mouthing his regret while the narrator has already moved on is a masterful use of the medium. This is AD at its best: narrative, character, and visual comedy working together.

jokes.text: Buster comes home from hospital. 'Let's all give me a big hand!' Family reacts with forced, horrified laughter.
jokes.setup: Buster arrives home after losing his hand. The family is trying to act normal.
jokes.punchline: 'Let's all give me a big hand!' — Buster makes the hand joke himself at the absolute worst moment, triggering collective cringe.
jokes.explanation: A hand joke that was inevitable but still lands because it's Buster delivering it himself with complete unawareness of why it's devastating. The forced laughter from the family is perfect cringe comedy. The moment is earned, specific, and quotable. High impact.

jokes.text: George Michael is sent to get Uncle Buster a bandage 'for his forehead. And while you're at it, get two.'
jokes.setup: Someone has been hit in the forehead, Michael asks George Michael to get a bandage.
jokes.punchline: 'And while you're at it, get two' — the casual request for twice as many bandages implies additional undisclosed injuries, delivered with total calm.
jokes.explanation: A small but effective deadpan beat. The understated 'and while you're at it, get two' is funnier than any explanation would be. Good economy.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Guess this means you don't wanna play "got your nose" with me.' Followed by forced laughing.
jokes.setup: Buster has arrived home missing his hand.
jokes.punchline: Lucille makes the 'got your nose' joke about a man who literally has no hand — a children's game involving pretending to grab a nose — delivered with her trademark icy timing.
jokes.explanation: A dark joke that reveals Lucille's character — she can't help making the wisecrack even at her son's expense. The 'got your nose' callback to children's games is an unexpected and sharp choice. Very Lucille.

jokes.text: Gob arrives and doesn't notice Buster's missing hand at first. 'Oh, my God! What—What happened to your— He's got no hand!'
jokes.setup: The family has already processed Buster's injury. Gob arrives late and oblivious.
jokes.punchline: Gob's delayed realization and stumbling freak-out — starting a sentence he can't finish — is a perfect delayed reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: Gob's obliviousness as a character trait deployed well here. His stuttering realization functions as both a reaction beat and a reminder that he's about to become the guilty party.

jokes.text: Gob's defense of seals: 'A seal would never prey on a human unless, of course, it had acquired a taste for mammal blood.'
jokes.setup: Gob hears that a seal bit off Buster's hand and is defending seals' honor.
jokes.punchline: He starts with what sounds like a genuine character defense, then adds the damning caveat about mammal blood — accidentally incriminating himself.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best character-comedy moments. The structure is perfect: confident defense → casual, devastating exception. The phrase 'acquired a taste for mammal blood' is grotesque and specific in a way that only Gob could make it. The audience realizes before the other characters do that Gob knows exactly what happened. High quotability.

jokes.text: Gob: 'How the hell would a seal know how to tie a bow tie anyway? I can barely tie the yellow one that I wear in my act.' Buster: 'This one had a yellow bow tie!'
jokes.setup: Gob questions how a seal could wear a bow tie, volunteering that he owns and struggles with a yellow bow tie.
jokes.punchline: Buster's reveal that the attacking seal had a yellow bow tie — the exact one Gob struggles to tie — is the escalating confirmation of Gob's guilt.
jokes.explanation: The comedic trap springs perfectly. Gob's self-incriminating defense reaches its logical conclusion when Buster casually provides the exact identifying detail. The structure is essentially a perfect comedic confession — Gob narrows the suspect pool to himself without realizing it. The timing of Buster's reveal is impeccable.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And later, Lucille could not contain her guilt any longer.' Lucille: 'I did this. I prayed that God take anything he wanted from us so that Buster wouldn't have to go to war.' Michael: 'Oh, Mom. After all these years, God's not gonna take a call from you.'
jokes.setup: Lucille confesses she prayed for God to take something from the family so Buster wouldn't have to serve.
jokes.punchline: Michael's dismissal — 'After all these years, God's not gonna take a call from you' — is both a character indictment of Lucille and an unexpected theological quip.
jokes.explanation: Michael's line is wonderfully constructed — framing God as someone who screens calls from Lucille is both funny and devastating about who Lucille is as a person. It implies years of transactional religion followed by divine abandonment. Strong character integration and a memorable image.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I did this. I think that the seal with the yellow bow tie might be the one that I released into the sea after giving it the taste for mammal blood.' Michael: 'Okay. You've got a better case than Mom does.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just claimed responsibility for Buster's injury through prayer. Gob now makes his own confession.
jokes.punchline: Michael's arbitration of who is MORE guilty — Lucille (prayer-based guilt) vs. Gob (released a blood-trained seal) — with the deadpan verdict 'You've got a better case than Mom does.'
jokes.explanation: Michael's casual guilt-arbitration between his mother and brother is a perfect comedic framing. 'You've got a better case than Mom does' treats familial culpability for dismemberment like a legal argument, which is both very Michael and very Bluth.

jokes.text: Gob: 'At the very least, I can remove that incriminating bow tie.'
jokes.setup: Gob volunteers to hunt down the seal and potentially retrieve Buster's hand for a transplant.
jokes.punchline: He adds as a fallback goal: removing the 'incriminating' bow tie from the seal — prioritizing evidence destruction over his brother's recovery.
jokes.explanation: The word 'incriminating' is doing all the work here — it's Gob's legal/self-preservation instinct applied to a seal's neckwear. Pure character comedy. The economy is excellent and it's highly quotable. Very Gob: his first practical concern is covering his tracks.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Tobias claim to know where their daughter Maeby is. 'She's with her debate club, and they're on their way to Sacremende for the semifinals.' Narrator: 'She wasn't. And a Google search of the word "Sacremende" only came up with this.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay confidently tells Michael she knows where Maeby is.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals 'Sacremende' doesn't exist — Lindsay made up a fake city name — and the Google search result gag implies it returned only Lindsay's invented reference.
jokes.explanation: The Google gag is inventive meta-comedy — using a contemporary tech reference to confirm how thoroughly Lindsay fabricated Maeby's whereabouts. 'Only came up with this' implies the single result is Lindsay's own lie, which is elegant. Strong originality for its era.

jokes.text: Maeby on the film set: 'What on earth is taking so long? Am I the only one who wants to get home and see their kids?' — a teenage girl who is herself a neglected child.
jokes.setup: Maeby is overseeing a professional film production she bluffed her way into running.
jokes.punchline: She expresses impatience about getting home to see 'their kids' — as a teenager who is herself neglected by her own parents — delivered with total self-unawareness.
jokes.explanation: Works on multiple levels: absurdity of Maeby as a film boss, irony that she references 'kids' when she herself is one who no one goes home to see. Classic AD irony layering.

jokes.text: Michael asks where 'your child' is. Lindsay and Tobias: 'I don't have a child, Michael.' / 'We don't have a child, Michael.' Then both realize: 'Did you mean Maeby?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Lindsay and Tobias where 'your child' is, trying to make a point about parental awareness.
jokes.punchline: Both Lindsay and Tobias independently deny having a child — they've each forgotten Maeby — before simultaneously realizing he meant their actual daughter.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed neglectful-parent joke. Both parents independently reaching the same conclusion (we have no child) before correcting themselves is structurally satisfying and deeply character-specific to Lindsay and Tobias. Their simultaneous 'oh' is a perfect reaction beat. High character integration — this joke only works because of who these two are.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'll find somebody who wants an adventure.' Then immediately: 'George Michael, how would you like to get away from Uncle Buster?' George Michael: 'I'd really like that.'
jokes.setup: Michael pretends to give up on finding a break-in accomplice, then immediately pivots to recruiting his teenage son.
jokes.punchline: He frames the illegal activity as 'getting away from Uncle Buster' — a relatable teen motivation — and George Michael agrees enthusiastically.
jokes.explanation: A good character moment that reveals Michael's manipulation of his son. The fact that George Michael's motivation for helping with a crime is simply 'getting away from Uncle Buster' is the right kind of mundane specificity.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'll find somebody who wants an adventure.' / [whispers to George Michael] 'George Michael, how would you like to get away from Uncle Buster?'
jokes.setup: Michael presents the investigation into Maggie as a fun 'adventure'
jokes.punchline: He immediately reveals the real pitch to George Michael is just escaping Uncle Buster — the 'adventure' framing was entirely self-serving
jokes.explanation: Michael's manipulation of his son — dressing up the misdirection as 'adventure' — is in character. The immediate whispered pivot to the real pitch is a quick, effective beat.

jokes.text: Michael can't fit through the doggy door: 'I can't fit through that doggy door. But I am gonna risk being caught out here. Be careful.'
jokes.setup: Michael has recruited George Michael to break into Maggie's house and sends him to the doggy door.
jokes.punchline: Michael's inability to fit through the doggy door means the adult sends the child in to do the dangerous part, then solemnly notes he is 'risking being caught out here' — an absurdly lesser risk.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Michael's self-congratulation for bearing the lesser of two risks. Good character comedy — Michael genuinely believes he's the brave one here.

jokes.text: George Michael retrieves a phone number taped to the outside of Maggie's door — he never even went inside. Michael: 'It's probably the Sparkletts guy.'
jokes.setup: Michael has elaborate plans for George Michael's break-in. George Michael comes back almost immediately.
jokes.punchline: The 'adventure' consists of reading a number off a Post-it note on the door. Michael immediately dismisses it as the water delivery company.
jokes.explanation: The anti-climax is well-executed. Michael's buildup to a covert adventure is deflated by the laziest possible outcome, and his immediate skeptical dismissal of the evidence he just obtained is classic self-defeating behavior.

jokes.text: Police officer informs Michael his partner is inside Maggie's house with his gun drawn, just as Michael claimed they were 'just waiting to see if Maggie's home.'
jokes.setup: Michael is casing Maggie's house. A cop pulls up and asks if everything is all right.
jokes.punchline: Michael gives an innocent explanation while the officer casually reveals his armed partner is inside the house Michael just had his son break into.
jokes.explanation: A competent cringe beat. The danger is communicated effectively and Michael's near-miss is appropriately nerve-wracking, but it doesn't have a strong punchline beyond 'they almost got caught.'

jokes.text: Gob at the dock: 'I'm lookin' for the seal that's got the taste for kitty blood.' He's surrounded by cats meowing.
jokes.setup: Gob arrives at the dock to hunt the seal that bit off Buster's hand.
jokes.punchline: His plan for catching a seal is to use cats as bait, and he's already surrounded by them. His modification of 'mammal blood' to 'kitty blood' is a wonderful Gob-ism.
jokes.explanation: 'I'll pay you handsomely' to a crowd of cats, plus the cat-bait scheme, is pure Gob absurdism. The escalating chaos of the cat scene is well-set-up. 'Kitty blood' is a genuinely funny word choice.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I'll pay you handsomely.' — to the meowing cats.
jokes.setup: Gob is negotiating with cats to serve as bait for the seal.
jokes.punchline: 'Handsomely' — a hand pun delivered to cats who cannot understand or appreciate it, while Gob is earnestly trying to hire them.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly placed pun that works on two levels: the standard meaning (paying generously) and the hand pun that's become the episode's running theme. Gob delivering it earnestly to cats makes it absurdist gold. Easy to miss on first watch — high rewatch value.

jokes.text: Gob: 'God! It's like herding cats!' — while literally herding cats on a boat.
jokes.setup: Gob's plan to use cats as seal bait has descended into chaos.
jokes.punchline: He uses the idiom 'it's like herding cats' while literally herding cats — the expression becomes literally accurate.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely brilliant joke. The idiom 'it's like herding cats' means 'it's impossibly chaotic,' and Gob uses it in exactly the right context — except he is literally, actually herding cats. The colloquial and literal meanings collide perfectly. High economy (five words), exceptional structure, and strong visual comedy. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Film crew on the camera boat: 'Why are there cats in the shot? This is the camera boat. Can you lose the [bleep] cats please?' [Maeby appears]
jokes.setup: Maeby is overseeing a film shoot on the water.
jokes.punchline: Gob's cat-filled seal-hunting boat has crashed into her movie shoot. Two completely separate Bluth disaster plots converge on the open water.
jokes.explanation: The collision of two absurd plotlines is a classic AD structural move. The film crew's exasperated 'Can you lose the cats?' is a great reaction beat.

jokes.text: Michael introduces the situation to George Michael: 'In there is the father of the child that Maggie wants me to help her raise. And he probably doesn't know that she's got another man involved.'
jokes.setup: Michael has tracked the phone number to an address and is preparing to confront the supposed rival father.
jokes.punchline: His framing positions himself as 'another man involved' with Maggie — not realizing the people inside are the gay couple who hired her as a surrogate.
jokes.explanation: A setup joke for the upcoming reveal — its value is in dramatic irony. On its own it's not the laugh, but it functions well as comedic foreshadowing. Rewatch value is high once the twist is known.

jokes.text: Michael confronts the address, expecting a romantic rival. The door is opened by a gay couple. 'There's a father. There's two fathers. David!' / 'I'm making gravy.' / 'That bitch is gonna keep our child.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been building up to confronting what he imagined was a secret boyfriend/rival. He knocks on the door.
jokes.punchline: He discovers the 'rival' is a gay married couple who hired Maggie as a surrogate — one of them is calmly making gravy.
jokes.explanation: A great misdirection payoff. The buildup has been to a romantic confrontation; the reveal is two men in a domestic scene, one making gravy. The mundane domestic detail ('I'm making gravy') against the dramatic stakes is the perfect tonal contrast. The twist reframes the entire episode's plot.

jokes.text: The song 'Hot Cops' plays as Michael is surrounded by Taylor and David at the door.
jokes.setup: Michael opens the door expecting trouble.
jokes.punchline: The 'Hot Cops' theme plays — a callback to the recurring stripper-cop bit in Arrested Development — as Taylor and David are revealed to be gay men, not actual cops.
jokes.explanation: The 'Hot Cops' musical sting is a reliable AD callback that always gets a laugh from fans. The placement here is well-timed and connects to the broader surrogate/gay couple storyline.

jokes.text: Taylor and David explain the surrogacy: 'In a funny way, neither do we. We just gave her a mixed cocktail. Two parts Taylor. Two parts Carter. And let her do her thing.'
jokes.setup: The gay couple explains they gave Maggie a 'mixed cocktail' of genetic material and don't know which of them is the biological father.
jokes.punchline: The clinical cocktail metaphor and their total serenity about not knowing paternity is delivered with warm matter-of-factness.
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist character work. The cocktail metaphor is clinical and odd, and their total peace with the ambiguity is a sweet comedic note. 'Two parts Taylor, two parts Carter' is a nice formulation.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I just hope you find out which one of you can breast-feed.' Then immediate embarrassed backtrack.
jokes.setup: Michael is being warm and supportive to the gay couple about not needing to know which is the biological father.
jokes.punchline: He makes a tone-deaf breast-feeding joke to two men, immediately regretting it. One of them delivers: 'Just because we don't have breasts does not mean we cannot nourish our child.'
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael fumble — his inability to not say the wrong thing, followed by genuine mortification. The dignified response from the couple ('does not mean we cannot nourish our child') treats the moment with appropriate gravity, which deepens the cringe.

jokes.text: David: 'Boy, you just cannot get off the breast thing, can you?' — at the later hospital scene.
jokes.setup: Michael keeps returning to breast-feeding jokes in conversation with the gay couple.
jokes.punchline: David's exasperated callback to the earlier exchange confirms it's become a pattern.
jokes.explanation: A solid callback that confirms Michael has repeated the same error. Good economy and timing; earns its laugh as a payoff.

jokes.text: Michael: 'For once, I'm one lie ahead of Maggie Lizer's lies.' Narrator: 'In fact, Michael was one lie behind her.'
jokes.setup: Michael is congratulating himself on finally being ahead of Maggie's deceptions.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately corrects him — Michael is still behind, as always.
jokes.explanation: Another perfect example of AD's narrator-as-ironic-commentator structure. The economy is excellent — Michael's self-congratulation and its immediate demolition happen in two sentences. The structure mirrors joke #26 (narrator correcting Michael's self-image) but here it's more plot-functional.

jokes.text: Maggie in court reveals Loretta is wearing a fat suit, then rips it off — and Loretta is actually just fat. Barry: 'Wow. Did I lose this one.'
jokes.setup: Maggie confidently reveals she has a receipt proving Loretta bought a fat suit — intending to show her fat is fake.
jokes.punchline: When Maggie removes the suit, Loretta is revealed to actually be fat. Barry's stunned 'Wow. Did I lose this one' is the deadpan capper.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed reversal — the audience has been primed to think the fat suit reveal is the twist, but there's a second twist. Barry's self-assessment ('Did I lose this one') is a perfect deadpan concession. The rewatch value is high because the whole fat suit plot has been building to this.

jokes.text: Judge: 'Mr. Zuckerkorn, you've been warned about touching.' Barry: 'You said spanking.' Judge: 'I want this case thrown out immediately.'
jokes.setup: Barry has apparently touched someone in court — consistent with his behavior at the bar.
jokes.punchline: Barry's defense is a semantic distinction — the judge said 'touching,' not 'spanking' — which is both legally absurd and consistent with his personality.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback to the bar spanking. Barry's split-hairs defense ('you said spanking') is exactly right for his character — incompetent in the worst possible way, while genuinely believing in his own logic.

jokes.text: Maggie says 'Quickly, Your Honor. Quickly throw it out' while Loretta's water breaks — begging the judge to dismiss the case before the surrogate baby is born in the courtroom.
jokes.setup: The fat suit reveal has gone wrong, Loretta's water breaks, and the surrogacy scheme is seconds from being exposed.
jokes.punchline: Maggie's urgent 'Quickly throw it out!' takes on a double meaning — 'throw out the case' and 'deliver the baby' — while she desperately tries to contain the chaos.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation comedy — Maggie's desperation to get the case dismissed before the baby is born in court is both plot-driven and funny in its layered urgency. The scene is building to maximum chaos.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It was Loretta's urine that Tobias and Lindsay ended up testing — a fact they missed when they were making out in the shower.'
jokes.setup: The narrator is explaining why the urine test came back positive for pregnancy.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay and Tobias were so distracted by making out in Maggie's shower that they tested the wrong woman's urine — their passion apparently reignited by breaking and entering.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best reveals — the urine test subplot is perfectly resolved with a detail that is absurd, character-specific, and retroactively recontextualizes the entire pregnancy plot. 'A fact they missed when they were making out in the shower' is a brilliantly deadpan delivery. The idea that breaking into someone's house reignited their romance is perfectly Tobias and Lindsay.

jokes.text: Gob tells Maeby: 'I think I'm responsible for Buster's hand.' Maeby: 'Yeah, and I'm responsible for an $80-million movie without an ending.' Gob: 'I know those problems seem big when you're a kid, Maeby.'
jokes.setup: Gob is confessing his guilt to Maeby, expecting empathy.
jokes.punchline: Maeby's problem — running an $80-million film — is objectively comparable to (or larger than) Gob's guilt. Gob condescends to her as 'a kid' anyway.
jokes.explanation: Both characters are perfectly rendered: Maeby's blasé '$80-million movie without an ending' as her parallel 'problem' is a wonderful comic juxtaposition, and Gob patronizing her as a child while she's running a studio is pure character comedy. The dismissal is the punchline, not the confession.

jokes.text: Gob tells Maeby to 'just tell him the truth' and 'he'll respect you for it.' Then: 'Okay, now I'm just lying for no reason.'
jokes.setup: Gob gives Maeby earnest advice about honesty and integrity.
jokes.punchline: He immediately admits the advice is false — not because he believes in lying, but because he's apparently just defaulted to lying as a habit even without strategic purpose.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest character-comedy moments. 'Now I'm just lying for no reason' is a perfect Gob line — it implies that his lying is usually purposeful, but here he's lied reflexively. It's a devastating self-portrait in one sentence. The character integration is near-perfect: only Gob would be self-aware about lying while still doing it. Highly quotable and deeply funny.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And back at the penthouse, Lucille was praying for the second time in her life.'
jokes.setup: The narrator sets up Lucille praying.
jokes.punchline: 'For the second time in her life' — implying the first was earlier in this episode, and in Lucille's entire existence she has prayed twice, both in one day, both self-serving.
jokes.explanation: A characteristically efficient narrator joke. One clause ('the second time in her life') tells us everything about Lucille's relationship with religion. The callback to the earlier prayer is seamless.

jokes.text: Lucille prays: 'Lord, let Buster forget everything. And if you need to take another hand to make that happen, please make it Gob's.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is guilt-ridden and praying for Buster to forget the truth about his father (Oscar) while also wanting him to forget the hand injury.
jokes.punchline: She negotiates with God to take Gob's hand as payment — casually requesting that one son be maimed to cover up her sins against another.
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of dark Lucille character comedy. She's praying — ostensibly a selfless act — but the content is pure selfishness and casual cruelty to Gob. 'Please make it Gob's' is her genuinely bargaining with God for a specific replacement victim. The character integration is nearly perfect: only Lucille would pray this prayer. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It was at that exact moment that Buster did forget he'd lost his hand.' — as Buster's prosthetic hook catches in something and he screams.
jokes.setup: Lucille has just prayed for Buster to forget his injury.
jokes.punchline: God literally answers — at the 'exact moment' — by having the hook catch violently, causing screaming that is the opposite of forgetting the hand.
jokes.explanation: The ironic precision of 'at that exact moment' combined with the opposite of the intended prayer result (Buster is not forgetting his hand) is a well-structured irony. The callback to Lucille's prayer is seamless and adds a comedic divine-logic layer.

jokes.text: Buster realizes the prosthetic hook has caught in something and screams: 'I'm a monster!'
jokes.setup: Buster catches his hook in something, causing chaos and screaming.
jokes.punchline: His reaction — 'I'm a monster!' — is an extreme but characteristically Buster emotional overcorrection, treating a momentary snag as a complete identity crisis.
jokes.explanation: Buster's 'I'm a monster!' is deeply character-specific — his emotional extremism applied to a physical awkwardness. The line is highly quotable and very Buster. The physical comedy implied is well-supported by the surrounding dialogue.

jokes.text: Gob confesses to Buster that the seal was his, gives a tearful truth-telling moment. Buster: 'You did this to me?' Gob: 'Yeah.' Narrator: 'And Gob was about to experience something that didn't feel that good.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just said 'Truth feels good' after confessing — a sincere character moment.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's immediate foreshadowing that something unpleasant is coming for Gob — implying Buster is about to attack him — perfectly deflates the earnestness.
jokes.explanation: A lovely structural joke — Gob's epiphany ('Truth feels good') is immediately set up for a violent refutation by the narrator. The anticipated Buster violence functions as a delayed punchline. Good use of narrator as comedic foreshadower.

jokes.text: George Michael is told to wait in the car outside the hospital. 'Don't smoke, sonny.' — from a dying/coughing elderly man.
jokes.setup: George Michael is waiting outside the hospital. An old man exits, clearly very ill.
jokes.punchline: The sick man offers the most ironic possible anti-smoking advice — while presumably demonstrating the consequences — delivered with a wheeze.
jokes.explanation: A quick, dark visual gag that works through economy. The dying man advising against smoking is a classic ironic beat. Low character integration (it's not really about any specific character) but the economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael in the hospital room with Maggie: 'Is this our little bundle of... two gay cops' baby?'
jokes.setup: Michael enters the delivery room expecting to see Maggie holding the baby he thought might be his.
jokes.punchline: He starts the traditional 'bundle of joy' opener, then trails off as reality catches up — 'two gay cops' baby' is his improvised correction.
jokes.explanation: The interrupted 'bundle of...' followed by 'two gay cops' baby' is a good comedic stutter that reveals Michael processing unexpected information in real time. His improvised terminology is funny in its awkwardness.

jokes.text: Maggie admits she outsourced the surrogacy after she couldn't get pregnant: 'Hey, I outsourced it, okay?'
jokes.setup: Maggie is confessing to Michael that she never actually carried the baby.
jokes.punchline: 'I outsourced it, okay?' — using corporate business language to describe farming out a pregnancy, delivered defensively.
jokes.explanation: 'Outsourced' applied to carrying a baby is a sharp, memorable word choice that captures Maggie's entire character — transactional, pragmatic, and unapologetic. The economy is excellent and the word is perfectly chosen. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Michael tells George Michael: 'You're the only son I've ever needed.' Bell dings. Narrator: 'That's all the adventure I've ever needed.' Then Michael sends George Michael to 'M. Main floor.' Narrator: 'But M didn't stand for main floor.'
jokes.setup: Michael has a touching reunion with George Michael and directs him to meet at 'M — Main floor.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately undermines the father-son moment: 'M' doesn't mean what Michael said. The warm moment is a setup for another deception.
jokes.explanation: Another example of Michael's self-image as 'the good guy' being instantly punctured by the narrator. The warmth of the father-son moment is set up to be immediately undercut, and 'but M didn't stand for main floor' is a good cliffhanger joke with episode-closing implications.

jokes.text: PA System: 'Dr. Ward to the morgue, please. Dr. Ward to the morgue, please. Stat.' [Tool whirring sound]
jokes.setup: Michael pressed 'M' thinking it was Main floor
jokes.punchline: The elevator opens to the morgue — a maximally dark destination for someone who just gave a heartfelt speech about adventure and family. 'Dr. Ward to the morgue, stat' plays in the background as dark ironic commentary.
jokes.explanation: The morgue as the destination for Michael's sentimental elevator moment is perfectly calibrated dark comedy — the show puncturing emotional closure with a deeply unfunny location. The PA announcement adds a layer of absurdist specificity.

jokes.text: Michael and Maggie say 'When we're together it's like—' Michael: 'Magic?' Maggie: 'I was gonna say train wreck.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie are having a romantic scene about how they make each other feel.
jokes.punchline: Michael guesses 'magic' — the clichéd romantic word — and Maggie corrects him with 'train wreck,' which is both more accurate and funnier.
jokes.explanation: A well-executed misdirection — Michael falls into the romantic-movie trap and Maggie's 'train wreck' is both a joke and an honest description of their entire relationship. Good economy and timing.

jokes.text: Michael and Maggie make a vow: 'No more lying. No deceit, no sex. Everything.' Narrator: 'But that, too, turned out to be a lie.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Maggie make a solemn vow to end their relationship and all its associated behaviors.
jokes.punchline: The narrator dismisses the vow immediately and entirely — not just one promise, but the whole vow is a lie.
jokes.explanation: A clean, efficient episode-closing irony. 'But that, too, turned out to be a lie' is a satisfying moral summation delivered without fanfare. The word 'too' is doing nice work — it situates the vow in a long chain of lies. Good economy.

jokes.text: On the next AD: Buster and Gob 'find themselves closer than ever' after Gob's hook is lodged in Buster's ass. Doctor: 'You've still got a hook in your ass.'
jokes.setup: The 'On the next...' teaser shows Buster and Gob apparently having resolved their conflict.
jokes.punchline: 'Closer than ever' is a literal description: Gob's hook is physically embedded in Buster's posterior. The doctor's deadpan 'you've still got a hook in your ass' is the delivery.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly constructed tag. 'Closer than ever' operates as a warm relationship description and a literal physical fact simultaneously. The doctor's clinical 'you've still got a hook in your ass' is perfect deadpan. This is a strong teaser gag that rewards attention to the earlier physical comedy.

jokes.text: Maggie's 'big surprise' in the next-episode teaser: she's apparently pregnant again (or still). 'You have got to be kidding me.'
jokes.setup: The 'On the next...' teaser shows Maggie with a new revelation.
jokes.punchline: Her exasperated 'You have got to be kidding me' — the exact reaction appropriate to the entire episode's pregnancy complications — continues the fake pregnancy / real pregnancy / surrogate loop.
jokes.explanation: A functional teaser beat — Maggie's exasperation is appropriate but the joke is underdeveloped as a standalone moment. Works as setup for the next episode more than as a punchline here.

tmdb_overview: Michael tries to find out who's the father of Maggie's baby, or if it's just another of her lies; Gob tries to find out whether he was responsible for Buster's accident.
insight: Escalation-driven episode hits 85.8 with 61 jokes—visual gags fuel Bluth family chaos.
